title: STUDIO Science — Covalent Bonding in Water

science_subject.phenomenon: How atoms share electrons in a water molecule
science_subject.key_measurement: O–H bond length ~0.96 Å; H–O–H angle ~104.5° (NIST WebBook)
science_subject.visualization_prompt: Scientific chemistry visualization: bent H2O molecule with shared electron pairs between oxygen and two hydrogens, neutral clinical grade, Lewis structure fidelity, no decorative glow

sources.citation: NIST Standard Reference Database 69 — NIST Chemistry WebBook, water (H2O)
Pauling L., The Nature of the Chemical Bond, 3rd ed. (Cornell University Press, 1960)

beats.speech_text: Water looks simple — two hydrogens, one oxygen. But the bond holding them together is not a handoff of electrons. It is a share.
In a covalent bond, atoms pool valence electrons into a shared region. In water, oxygen pulls harder on those electrons, bending the molecule and making the O–H bonds polar.
Spectroscopy and crystallography pin the geometry: an H–O–H angle near one hundred four degrees and bond lengths under one ångström — tabulated in the NIST Chemistry WebBook.
See the payoff: two equivalent O–H σ bonds in a bent triatomic frame — shared electron density, not isolated spheres glued together.
beats.visualization_prompt: Bent water molecule with shared electron pair lobes on O–H bonds, clinical accurate color, chemistry @2 plate fidelity

beats.speech_text: That shared-electron geometry is why water is polar, why ice floats, and why life chemistry runs in solution — bonding at the root.
